title: The Garden of Forking Paths
originalTitle: El jardín de senderos que se bifurcan
author: Jorge Luis Borges
authorBio: Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986) was an Argentine short-story writer, essayist, poet and translator, and a key figure in Spanish-language and universal literature. His work embraces the character of unreality in all literature.
translatorNote: Translating Borges requires navigating the labyrinth of his precise, yet ambiguous vocabulary. I aimed to capture the philosophical weight of his original Spanish while keeping the English fluid.
excerpt: A short story about time, espionage, and the infinite.
text:
On leaving, I discussed with my host the details of his work. He explained to me that the book he was writing was not a simple sequential narrative, but a labyrinth of forking paths. Every time a character made a decision, the universe split into multiple parallel realities...

(This is a short excerpt translated for the project to demonstrate the complexity of narrative structure.)

title: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair: Poem I
originalTitle: Veinte poemas de amor y una canción desesperada
author: Pablo Neruda
authorBio: Pablo Neruda (1904–1973) was a Chilean poet-diplomat and politician. He is considered one of the greatest and most influential poets of the 20th century and won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1971.
translatorNote: Neruda's musicality is both his greatest strength and the translator's greatest challenge. I focused on preserving the rhythm and emotional urgency of the original over strict literal accuracy.
excerpt: One of Neruda's most celebrated love poems, translated anew.
text:
Tonight I can write the saddest lines.

Write, for example: 'The night is shattered and the blue stars shiver in the distance.'

The wind of the night turns in the sky and sings. Tonight I can write the saddest lines. I loved her, and sometimes she loved me too...

(This is a short excerpt translated for the project.)

title: The Metamorphosis
author: Franz Kafka
authorBio: Franz Kafka (1883–1924) was a German-speaking Bohemian novelist and short-story writer, widely regarded as one of the major figures of 20th-century literature. His work, such as The Trial and The Castle, explores themes of alienation and absurdity.
translatorNote: Kafka's German is deceptively plain. The horror of the story emerges not from dramatic language but from the bureaucratic flatness with which the extraordinary is described. I worked hard to maintain that unsettling calm.
excerpt: A new translation of Kafka's landmark work on alienation.
text:
One morning, when Gregor Samsa woke from troubled dreams, he found himself transformed in his bed into a horrible vermin. He lay on his armour-like back, and if he lifted his head a little he could see his brown belly, slightly domed and divided by arches into stiff sections...

(This is a short excerpt translated for the project.)
